---
title: "?"
subtitle: " Advice for diagramming"
---

# Introduction

Diagrams are really important. A good diagram quickly conveys the high-level idea of your approach to readers before they dig deep into the methodology of the papoer. When people read your paper, they will usually look at your abstract, introduction, tables, and your main diagram before they dig deeper into the details of your methodology.

Diagramming style heavily depends on the venue that you try to publish at. For example, in clinical venues, the diagram should emphasise how clinicians or medical end users can make use of your contribution (e.g. dataset, new model architecture) in their real-world situations. (pls add more egs). You should always look at the diagrams of previous successful papers published at your target venue to understand aspects like how detailed your diagram should be, what visual style reviewers like, and how much explanation you should include in your diagram.

# What your diagram should and shouldn't do, and some general tips
1. Your diagram is not just a repetition of whatever your wrote in the text. Your diagram should be able to convey how your method works faster than yours readers reading through your text description of the method

2. Your diagram should make your contribution extremely obvious. The reader should be able to explain the high-level idea of what your contribution is after looking at your diagram.

3. The flow of information should be obvious. All entities (e.g. boxes, arrows, losses, prediction results) should be labelled clearly

4. Do not waste time and space explaining standard components (e.g. softmax, binary cross-entropy). Reviewers are experienced and already know this stuff.

5. Using visual language can be helpful (for example, your can use green to denote positive model behaviour and red for undesirable model behaviour)
